NorthBridge Bank — Module 1, Lesson 2
When Systems Fail: Understanding Service Dependencies
Follow the engineering team’s investigation — Monday morning, 09:15
Investigation:
Firewall WAF / LB Web Server App Server Database
📱 09:15 — Monday Morning Login Failed Rahul opens NorthBridge. The page loads. He clicks Login. Nothing.

It is Monday morning, 09:15. Rahul opens the NorthBridge Bank app on his phone. The login page loads normally. He types his Customer ID and password. He clicks Login.

“Login Failed. Please try again later.”

He tries again. Same message. He tries a third time. Same message. He is not the only one — but he does not know that yet.

This error is the surface symptom. It tells us where the failure is visible. It does not tell us where the failure started. That is the difference a security engineer must always hold in mind.

⚠ Widespread Failure 847 Customers Affected Same error. Every device. Every city. Simultaneously.

847 customers are seeing the same “Login Failed” message at exactly the same time, across every device and every city.

The support line is overwhelmed. The engineering team receives an alert. The incident clock starts.

Here is what the scale already tells us: when the same error appears across every device, every network, and every location simultaneously, it eliminates the customer’s device, their internet connection, and their credentials.

The failure is inside the bank. It is in a component that every customer request passes through. The investigation begins.

⚠ Incident Alert — 09:15 The Incident Monday morning — 847 customers cannot log in

It is 09:15 on Monday morning. The NorthBridge customer support line is overwhelmed. The error is always the same: Login Failed — Please try again later. The website loads. The login page loads. But the moment customers submit their credentials — nothing.

The engineering team receives the incident alert. The investigation clock starts. Their job: work through the chain, eliminate what is healthy, find what is broken.

Before they begin, one concept is essential: every component in the NorthBridge chain depends on the components downstream from it. If any one link breaks, every link above it fails too — even if those components are perfectly healthy. This is called a service dependency. The customer sees the error at the surface. The cause may be anywhere in the chain.

✓ Cleared — Traffic passing through The Firewall Security boundary — allow or block, nothing else

The team checks the Firewall first — it is the outermost layer. The Firewall inspects every incoming connection and makes a single binary decision: allow it or block it. It checks source IP, destination port, and protocol against its ruleset. It does not read request content. It does not know what a login is.

Don’t worry about what an IP address, destination port, protocol, or a ruleset is as of now — these will be covered in subsequent chapters in detail as we move forward in the course.

The evidence: customers can reach the NorthBridge website. That means packets are getting through the Firewall. The Firewall is cleared. The team moves inward.

✓ What a healthy Firewall looks like from the outside

Customer traffic reaches the NorthBridge website normally — pages load, requests arrive at the next layer. There are no unusual connection blocks in the logs and response times at the network edge are within normal range. If the Firewall were failing, customers would not see the website at all. They would get a timeout or a "This site cannot be reached" message before any page loaded. The fact that the website opens is your evidence that the Firewall is healthy.

Failure Mode — The Firewall
How it fails Hardware fault, software crash, or a misconfigured ruleset that blocks all traffic. Can also fail “open” — the ruleset disappears and unfiltered internet traffic reaches the internal network.
Customer sees The website does not load at all. Browser shows a connection timeout or “site cannot be reached.” This affects every customer simultaneously, across all devices and locations.
What’s affected Everything behind it: WAF, Load Balancer, Web Servers, App Server, Database. All unreachable. The entire bank goes dark from the outside.
Investigation clue If the website loads — even the homepage — the Firewall is not the problem. It is passing traffic. This is the first and fastest elimination in any incident.
✓ Cleared — Traffic reaching Web Servers WAF & Load Balancer Deep inspection and traffic distribution — inside the DMZ

Both devices sit inside the DMZ. The WAF (Web Application Firewall) looks inside the request — scanning for SQL injection, cross-site scripting, and known attack patterns the network Firewall cannot see. The Load Balancer distributes traffic across multiple Web Servers so no single server is overwhelmed. If one Web Server slows down or fails, the Load Balancer routes around it automatically.

The evidence: the website homepage and static pages load normally. That means the WAF has passed the traffic and the Load Balancer is successfully reaching Web Servers. Both are cleared. The team moves deeper.

✓ What a healthy WAF & Load Balancer looks like from the outside

Web pages load normally and traffic is spread evenly across all three Web Servers. The WAF logs show requests being passed through — not blocked. The Load Balancer shows all three servers receiving traffic. If the WAF were blocking requests, pages would return error codes instead of content. If the Load Balancer were down, no pages would load at all — not just login, but the homepage and every other page too. Pages loading is the signal that both layers are healthy.

Failure Mode — WAF & Load Balancer
WAF fails Either all traffic is blocked (website unreachable), or the WAF passes through malicious requests it should have caught. A misconfigured WAF rule can also block legitimate traffic, making the site appear broken for specific request types.
LB fails Traffic that passed the Firewall and WAF has nowhere to go. All three Web Servers are healthy — but unreachable. The website goes down even though nothing is wrong with the servers themselves.
Customer sees Website does not load. Same symptom as a Firewall failure — connection timeout. The difference is visible only in server logs, not to customers.
Investigation clue If static pages load but dynamic requests (like login) fail, the Load Balancer is likely routing correctly to Web Servers. The problem is deeper. If nothing loads, check WAF and LB before going further.
✓ Cleared — Static pages loading normally The Web Servers Front door of the bank — receives, routes, and responds

The Web Servers are the first systems inside the bank’s trusted infrastructure. Their job is to receive the request, handle the HTTP connection, and route it to the Application Server. They serve static content (homepage, help pages, contact pages) directly — they do not need the App Server for this.

NorthBridge runs three Web Servers in parallel. The Load Balancer distributes traffic across all three. If one fails, the other two continue serving customers without interruption. This is redundancy in practice.

The evidence: the homepage loads. Static pages load. Web Servers are responding. Cleared. But login still fails — which means the problem is in the systems the Web Server hands off to.

✓ What healthy Web Servers look like from the outside

All static content — the homepage, help pages, images, and the login page itself — loads quickly and correctly. Response times are in the normal range. The login page loading is your direct evidence that the Web Servers are healthy. A Web Server failure would show up as pages not loading, partial page loads, or intermittent errors. The fact that the login page renders completely means every Web Server in the pool is doing its job. The failure must be somewhere they hand off to.

Failure Mode — Web Servers
One server fails The Load Balancer detects it is unresponsive and routes all traffic to the remaining two servers. Customers notice nothing. This is redundancy working as designed.
All servers fail The Load Balancer has nowhere to send traffic. The website becomes unreachable. Browser shows connection errors. Same visible symptom as a Load Balancer failure.
Partial failure A Web Server can serve static pages but fail to forward requests to the App Server. Customers see the homepage but get errors when they try to log in or interact with their account.
Investigation clue If static content loads but interactive features fail, the Web Server is receiving and routing — the failure is downstream. If nothing loads at all, check Load Balancer and Web Servers together.
⚠ Suspect — Login processing fails here The Application Server Authentication, authorisation — the logic layer

The Application Server is where login processing happens. It receives the request from the Web Server and runs the logic: authenticate (is this really Rahul?) and authorise (is Rahul allowed to see this account?). To do this, it must query the Customer Database.

The evidence: login fails. The App Server is attempting to process the login but something is preventing it from completing. The team has two suspects now: the App Server itself is broken, or the App Server is healthy but cannot reach what it depends on — the Database.

The App Server cannot tell the customer why login failed. It only knows it could not complete the request. It returns a generic error: Login Failed — Please try again later.

✓ What a healthy App Server looks like from the outside

Login requests complete successfully — users are authenticated, sessions are created, and customers reach their accounts. The App Server logs show completed transactions with no timeout entries, and response times for login are within the normal threshold. A truly broken App Server would show process crashes or no response at all — not timeout logs pointing at a downstream dependency. Timeout logs mean the App Server is alive and working correctly — it is simply waiting for something below it that is not responding. That distinction is what tells the team the fault lies deeper in the chain.

Failure Mode — Application Server
How it fails Process crash, memory exhaustion, unhandled exception in the application code, or a failed software deployment. The App Server process stops responding to requests from the Web Server.
Customer sees Login fails. Static pages still load. Any action that requires processing (login, balance check, transfer) returns an error. Actions that only need the Web Server (loading the homepage) still work.
What’s affected All authenticated actions. The Database is untouched — the App Server is simply not sending it queries. The Database appears healthy in monitoring even though customers cannot log in.
vs Database failure Both produce identical symptoms for customers. The difference is visible in App Server logs: if the App Server is failing, it logs its own errors. If it is healthy and waiting, it logs “database timeout” errors instead.
⚠ Root Cause — Database offline The Database The crown jewel — found at the end of the chain

The App Server logs tell the story: database connection timeout. The Application Server is healthy, running, and receiving requests from the Web Server. But every time it tries to query the Customer Database — to verify Rahul’s credentials — it gets no response.

The Database has gone offline. The App Server cannot verify any login without it. Every login attempt across every Web Server returns the same error: Login Failed — Please try again later.

Root cause found. The failure is at the deepest point in the chain. The symptom surfaced at the top. Four healthy components — Firewall, WAF/LB, Web Servers, App Server — all reported the failure as if it were their own, because each one depends on the one below it.

✓ What a healthy Database looks like from the outside

When the Database is healthy, login requests complete in milliseconds — customers click the button and they are in. The App Server logs show no timeout entries, the connection pool is not exhausted, and there are no crash events in the Database logs. The clearest sign of a healthy Database is that you never think about it at all — everything above it simply works. When it fails, as it did here, the failure propagates upward silently through every layer until it reaches the customer as a generic error message, with no indication of where the real problem started.

Failure Mode — Database (Full Cascade)
How it fails Hardware fault, storage failure, out-of-memory crash, or a runaway query that locks the database process. The Database stops responding to new connections.
Step 1 Database stops responding to queries.
Step 2 App Server sends a login query. Receives no response. Times out after several seconds.
Step 3 App Server cannot verify credentials. Returns an error to the Web Server.
Step 4 Web Server forwards the error to the customer’s browser.
Step 5 Customer sees: “Login Failed — Please try again later.” The root cause is 5 steps removed from the visible symptom.
What’s affected All authenticated actions. Static pages still load. Only features requiring database queries fail — login, balance check, transaction history.
CIRCLE 7 OF 7 — SYNTHESIS Reading the Evidence Every working component is a clue — not just the broken one

The team found the Database failure in under ten minutes. Not by checking everything — by reading what was already working.

Website loads → Firewall: cleared.
Website loads → WAF & Load Balancer: cleared.
Static pages load → Web Servers: cleared.
Login fails → App Server or Database: suspect.
App Server logs show DB timeout → Database: root cause.

Five components. Four eliminated by evidence that was already visible. One found by following the chain to its end.

The key insight: the error a customer sees tells you where the failure surfaced. It does not tell you where it started. Engineers read what is working because working components are evidence too — they tell you exactly where the failure is not.

Investigation Methodology — Outside In
Rule 1 Start at the outermost layer. Work inward. Each cleared component narrows the search.
Rule 2 What is working is evidence. A loading homepage eliminates the Firewall, WAF, Load Balancer, and Web Servers simultaneously.
Rule 3 The customer’s error message tells you where the failure surfaced — not where it started. Always trace to the root.
Rule 4 When two suspects remain, check the logs of the upstream component first. If its logs say “timeout waiting for downstream,” the downstream component is the root cause.
In Lesson 3 This outside-in logic becomes a formal framework — a structured troubleshooting process you can apply to any incident, not just this one.
Circle 1 of 7
Engineering Principle

Analogy
